coredata.dc:title: Multiple heteroatom dopant carbon dots as a novel photoluminescent probe for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in living cells and environmental sample analysis

coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ion pollution harms human health and the environment and continues to worsen. Here, we report the synthesis of boron (B), phosphorous (P), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (BP/NS-CDs) by a one-step facile hydrothermal process. The optimum synthetic parameters are of 180 °C temperature, 12 h reaction time and 15% of PBA mass. The as-synthesized BP/NS-CDs exhibits excellent water solubility, strong green photoluminescence (PL) at 510 nm, and a high quantum yield of 22.4%. Moreover, BP/NS-CDs presented high monodispersity (7.2 ± 0.45 nm), excitation-dependent emission, PL stability over large pH, and high ionic strength. FTIR, XRD, and XPS are used to confirm the successful B and P doping of BP/NS-CDs. BP/NS-CD photoluminescent probes are selectively quenched by Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions but showed no response to the presence of other metal cations. The PL emission of BP/NS-CDs exhibited a good linear correlation with Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations with detection limits of 0.18 μM and 0.27 μM for Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Furthermore, the HCT116 survival cells kept at 99.4 ± 1.3% and cell imaging capability, when the BP/NS-CDs concentration is up to 300 μg/mL by MTT assay. The proposed sensor is potential applications for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in environmental water samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ion pollution harms human health and the environment and continues to worsen. Here, we report the synthesis of boron (B), phosphorous (P), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (BP/NS-CDs) by a one-step facile hydrothermal process. The optimum synthetic parameters are of 180 °C temperature, 12 h reaction time and 15% of PBA mass. The as-synthesized BP/NS-CDs exhibits excellent water solubility, strong green photoluminescence (PL) at 510 nm, and a high quantum yield of 22.4%. Moreover, BP/NS-CDs presented high monodispersity (7.2 ± 0.45 nm), excitation-dependent emission, PL stability over large pH, and high ionic strength. FTIR, XRD, and XPS are used to confirm the successful B and P doping of BP/NS-CDs. BP/NS-CD photoluminescent probes are selectively quenched by Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions but showed no response to the presence of other metal cations. The PL emission of BP/NS-CDs exhibited a good linear correlation with Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations with detection limits of 0.18 μM and 0.27 μM for Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Furthermore, the HCT116 survival cells kept at 99.4 ± 1.3% and cell imaging capability, when the BP/NS-CDs concentration is up to 300 μg/mL by MTT assay. The proposed sensor is potential applications for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in environmental water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal pollution from natural sources and anthropogenic activities poses serious problems (Mitra et al., 2022). Individual health is now seriously threatened by high levels of heavy metal ions in drinking water. Exposure to iron, copper, lead, cadmium and other metal ions has deleterious effects on bones and kidneys, wreaks havoc on neurological and central nervous systems, and causes hypertension (Jaishankar et al., 2014). Due to their physiological effects, Cu2+ and Fe3+ are of particular concern (Lorentzon et al., 2020). Exposure to Cu2+ is linked to neurological conditions such as spongiform encephalopathy, Wilson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease (Ejaz et al., 2020). According to United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) guidelines, drinkable water must not contain a Cu2+ content of ≥20 μM (Mahajan et al., 2019), and thus, Cu2+ concentration in drinking water must be regulated. In the human body, Fe3+ is the most abundant transition metal ion (Zheng et al., 2020), and is essential for normal functioning. Accordingly, iron deficiencies or excesses present serious health problems such as endotoxemia, hepatic cirrhosis, and Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease (Sadrzadeh and Saffari, 2004). Mass spectrometry (Mollah et al., 2000), electrochemical techniques (Ding et al., 2021), and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (Zhang et al., 2018) are frequently employed to detect metal ions but are not widely used because of the costs involved (Gupta et al., 2018; Tcyrulnikov et al., 2019). On the other hand, fluorescence sensors can detect metal ions cheaply with rapid response times and high sensitivities (De Acha et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2022). Due to their involvement in biological and environmental processes (Ganesan et al., 2022; Varadharajan et al., 2020, 2022), the identification and detection of metal ions using chemical sensors have become important research objectives (Ai, 2014; Zheng et al., 2020). However, from a developmental perspective, it is still difficult to construct fluorescent sensors that can distinguish between different analytes.

Due to their numerous possible applications, special optical features, biocompatibility, exceptional luminescence properties, and low toxicities, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much research attention (Liu et al., 2020; B. Wang et al., 2022). To date, CDs have been used for sensors (Li et al., 2019), bioimaging (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019), electrocatalysis (Tian et al., 2021), photocatalysis (Jung et al., 2022), drug delivery (Zeng et al., 2016), and in optoelectronic applications (Stepanidenko et al., 2021). The sizes, compositions, and surface chemistries of CDs are the three key parameters that influence their optical and electrical properties (Ding et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). Functionalization provides an effective strategy for adjusting the chemical and physical characteristics of CDs, especially by surface modification and intrinsic heteroatom doping (Li and Dong, 2018; Park et al., 2016). The stabilities of CDs are problematic in numerous applications, though they have been improved by time-consuming post-surface treatments. Doping techniques are used to increase CD fluorescence, and due to their abilities to modify the physical and chemical properties of CDs, elements like boron, nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus are being actively investigated (Das et al., 2021). Heteroatom doping techniques have been developed to increase quantum yields (QYs) and alter the optical characteristics of CDs (excitation-dependent or -independent emission) (Wu et al., 2019), and nitrogen, phosphorous (electron donors), sulfur, and boron (electron acceptors) doping techniques have been described (Sohal et al., 2021). Substitution of CDs by boron and/or phosphorous alters the chemical stability and dramatically impacts the physical, optical, and chemical properties of CDs (Wu et al., 2019; Barman et al., 2014). Several researchers have demonstrated the benefits of boron (B), phosphorous (P), sulfur (S), and metal ion doping (Barman et al., 2014; Chandra et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2019; Li et al., 2017; M. Wang et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2019). In particular, CDs doped with heteroatoms have been successfully employed as fluorescence probes to detect metal ions (Bian et al., 2017; Mohammed and Omer, 2020; Molkenova and Atabaev, 2019; M. C. Rong et al., 2017; Sadhanala et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019, 2017).

In the present study, CDs were successfully doped with boron (B), phosphorous (P), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S). The synthesis was carried out using a one-pot hydrothermal reaction and 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, phenylboronic acid, and phosphoric acid as precursors. The as-prepared BP/NS-CDs emitted green fluorescence with a QY of 22.4%. Using BP/NS-CDs PL was efficiently quenched by Cu2+ or Fe3+ in PBS at pH∼7.4. More importantly, the as-synthesized BP/NS-CDs exhibited highly selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in environmentally spiked water samples. In addition, we discuss the mechanism of the interaction between BP/NS-CDs and Cu2+ and Fe3+ (Scheme 1 ).

2,4-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (2,4-DABSA), phenylboronic acid (PBA), and phosphoric acid (PA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Metal nitrates and chloride salts were obtained from Merck. Deionized water and analytical grade reagents (used as received) were used throughout the study.

BN-CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method, as previously described with minor modifications (Barman et al., 2014; Kumar et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). The design of experiments and the optimization of BP/NS-CDs synthesis conditions are given in supplementary information (Fig. S1). Briefly, PBA (0.1 g) and 2,4-DABSA (0.08 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of water, and 2.0 mL of PA was then mixed with constant stirring at room temperature (RT). This mixture was then placed in an autoclave connected to a hydrothermal-assisted synthesizer and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Then cooling with RT and centrifuging for 15 min at 10,000 rpm, the BP/NS-CDs solution was collected, dialyzed (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 12 h to remove unreacted starting materials and then dried with vacuum oven at 60 °C for 48 h. The BP/NS-CDs obtained were then stored at 4 °C until required.

UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a UV 3220 spectrometer (Optizen) and an F-2700 spectrophotometer (HITACHI). The BP/NS-CDs were irradiated further by a hand UV lamp with the wavelength of 365 nm for different time. The irradiation powder intensity was about 344 μW/cm2. In addition, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer was performed (4000–400 cm−1). The diffraction study was conducted using a 5° min−1 scanning speed with a wavelength of 1,5405 Å was performed with a 10–80° range of X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (PANalytic X'Pert Philips, MRD model). High resolution and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM and TEM) study were obtained using a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL; 200 kV) by dropping an aqueous solution of BP/NS-CDs onto a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at RT. Surface and core compositions of BP/NS-CDs were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific, USA) Thermo Fisher, USA) study attained by a Multilab-2000 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα monochromatized source.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared BP/NS-CDs was determined using previously described (Mohandoss et al., 2021a), by dissolving BP/NS-CDs in deionized water (refractive index, n = 1.33) at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Quantum yield (QY) was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 (Hitachi).

The selectivity of BP/NS-CDs for Ag+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Ti3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sr2+, and Fe3+ were investigated. Metal ion solutions (1.975 mL, 50 μM) and BP/NS-CDs solutions (25 μL, 0.1 μg/mL) were added to transferred a 3 mL quartz cuvette. To determine whether the other metal ions inhibited the quenching effects of Cu2+ and Fe3+, the BP/NS-CDs solution was mixed with Cu2+ Fe3+ ions (0–50 μM) at RT. Emission and excitation slits were adjusted to 5 nm.

3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was utilized to evaluate the effects of BP/NS-CDs on HCT-116 cell viability. Briefly, cells were plated (5 × 103 cells/well) in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C in a humid, 5% CO2 and 95% air atmosphere. The culture standard was then replaced with 100 μL of fresh Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) holding BP/NS-CDs at 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, or 300 μg/mL. After an additional incubation for 24 or 48 h, 10 μL of PBS containing MTT (5 mg/mL) reagent was added to each well. In 24-well plates, 5 × 105 HCT-116 cells were cultivated on cover glasses and given 24 h to attach (Standard conditions). After removing supernatants, 150 μL of DMSO was mixed to dissolve the formazan. HCT-116 cells were treated for 60 min in complete cell media containing a 1 mg/mL solution of BP/NS-CDs (300 μg/mL) as described for above-mixed solutions. Cu2+ and Fe3+ were then added to cells and incubated for 12 h. Cells were then carefully washed with PBS (pH 7.0) and subjected to confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

Tap and lake water samples collected from different sources were spiked with known amounts of Cu2+ or Fe3+. Samples were filtered before analysis using Whatman filter paper to remove any solid and spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (1, 5, or 10 μM). Before testing, solutions were allowed to stand for 2 min at RT. Sample PL intensities were measured after spiking with standard Cu2+/Fe3+ solutions.

In this study, we report the facile one-step synthesis of BP/NS-CDs using 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, phenylboronic acid, and phosphoric acid as precursors. The as-prepared BP/NS-CDs were subjected to UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy. Prior to applying BP/NS-CDs for further studies, several amount (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 μg/mL) of the originally obtained BP/NS-CDs were carried out, as shown in Fig. S2. It could be seen that the intensity wavelength and the PL emission of the BP/NS-CDs were highly dependent on the concentration of BP/NS-CDs solution. As shown in Fig. S2, the emission intensity was highest with the addition of 0.1 μg/mL dilution factor, with emission maxima of 510 nm. In this observation, we studied the UV–Vis spectrum of BP/NS-CDs exhibited a broad peak at 450 nm, a small hump at 292 nm, and a sharp peak at 245 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The broad peak at 450 nm was presented to the n−π* transition of CO (Huo et al., 2021), CN groups (Pan et al., 2021), and the sharp peak to π–π* transition of core (CC) (Huo et al., 2021).

The PL spectrum of BP/NS-CDs contained a prominent peak at 510 nm with a Stokes shift of 60 nm upon excitation at 450 nm (green line). Pale yellow aqueous solutions of BP/NS-CDs shown in the inset of Fig. 1(a) produced green PL when exposed to 365 nm UV light. Fig. 1(b) displays the excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of BP/NS-CDs, which showed maximum emission at 510 nm (excitation wavelength at 450 nm). The QY of BP/NS-CDs was calculated at 22.4%, indicating outstanding PL characteristics (Liu et al., 2019). The PL intensity of BP/NS-CD improved as the varied excitation wavelength from 330 to 450 nm and then progressively fell from 450 to 500 nm. This observation showed that the inclusion of boron and phosphorous or boric acid/phosphoric acid groups altered the PL properties (intensity and emission wavelength) of BP/NS-CDs.

BP/NS-CD PL stability was investigated by varying pH and NaCl concentration and using different solvents (Fig. 2 ). PL intensity was strong and stable throughout the pH range 5–12, as previously reported (Ren et al., 2019). At pH values > 12 or <3, PL intensities gradually declined and solution colors changed. The stability of BP/NS-CDs at high NaCl ionic strength up to 1.0 M and accompanying color variations at different salt concentrations (Fig. 2(b,e)). The PL intensities of BP/NS-CDs were almost unchanged in NaCl concentrations up to 1.0 M, as previously reported (Mohammed and Omer, 2020), and the wavelength of PL emission has been reported to be significantly influenced by solvent CDs interactions (Mohammad-Jafarieh et al., 2021).

To explore the effects of solvents on BP/NS-CDs, we used acetonitrile, 1,4-dioxane, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, ethyl acetate, methanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and water (arranged in order of increasing polarity). Solvents also differ in terms of their protic properties (Lee et al., 2019). In ethanol, BP/NS-CDs had a higher emission intensity than in other solvents, as previously reported (Zhao et al., 2019). Color changes exhibited by BP/NS-CDs in different solvents are shown in Fig. 2(c,f). Finally, we measured the PL intensity of the BP/NS-CDs by UV light irradiation for different time (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. S3, the emission spectra of BP/NS-CDs after UV light irradiation for 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 h and the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths near 450/510 nm. The emission intensity when excited at 450 nm is reduced slowly with the increase of the irradiation time. BP/NS-CDs exhibits a significant red shift after the UV light irradiation for 1.5 h and a small red shift after longer time irradiation when excited at 450 nm. This result is good agreement with the excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of BP/NS-CDs (Fig. 1(b)). Thus, the as-prepared BP/NS-CDs showed remarkable PL stability over wide pH and NaCl concentrations, various solvents, and UV lamp irradiation time, which suggested their suitability in challenging environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological properties of BP/NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface and morphological properties of BP/NS-CDs were investigated by FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS (Figs. 3 and 4
). FT-IR spectra were obtained to detect functional groups on the surfaces of BP/NS-CDs (Fig. 3(a)). BP/NS-CDs exhibited absorption peaks at 3310, 3086, and 2991 cm−1, consistent to O–H, N–H, and C–H stretch vibrations, respectively, of hydroxyl and amine groups, (Zhao et al., 2017). Characteristic peaks at 2433, 1634, 1458, 1411, 1378, 1276, 1088, 1043, and 620 cm−1 were assigned to S–H stretch, CO/CC stretch, asymmetric CH2 bending vibration, C–S and B–O–H bending vibrations, B–C, C–O–C/C–OH, and B–O stretch, and –SO3H stretch, respectively, (Wu et al., 2019).

Additionally, P–O and PO stretching vibrations were detected at 880 and 1317 cm−1, respectively, confirming the presence of phosphorus, as previously reported (Shangguan et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2019). FT-IR confirmed that the BP-doped NS-CDs had abundant N and S groups. Fig. 3(b) shows the XRD pattern of BP/NS-CDs containing broad peaks at 2θ = 23.6°, corresponding to the graphene-like structure of BP/NS-CDs, which concurs with a previous report (Horax et al., 2017). TEM analysis of BP/NS-CDs were nearly spherical and most were well separated (Fig. 3(c)). HRTEM images of individual BP/NS-CDs showed a typical lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the 100 lattice plane spacing of graphene (Tian and Yin, 2019). BP/NS-CDs had an average particle size of 7.2 ± 0.45 nm (range 3.0–9.0 nm) as previously reported (He et al., 2021) Fig. 3(d).

XPS spectra of BP/NS-CDs survey scan showed the presence of nitrogen (N1s), sulfur (S2p), boron (B1s), phosphorous (P2p), carbon (C1s), and oxygen (O1s) with peaks at 400, 154, 192, 135, 285, and 532 eV (Kumar et al., 2019), respectively (Fig. 4(a)). The B1s spectrum in Fig. 4(b) two peaks at 190.8 and 191.6 eV, which is attributed to the B–N and B–O groups, respectively (Barman et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2019). The XPS spectrum of BP/NS-CD also showed three peaks at 133.9, 134.3, and 135.1 eV, which were assigned to P2p3/2, P2p1/2, and P–O, respectively (Barman et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2019) in Fig. 4(c). The N1s (Fig. 4(d)) demonstrated to N–C (399.8 eV), CN (400.5 eV), and N–H (401.8 eV) groups (Wu et al., 2019). The S2p spectrum in Fig. 4(e) shows three main bands at 163.5 eV, 165.3 eV, and 168.8 eV, which were corresponding to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, and S–O, respectively. The C1s spectrum contained three peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.3 eV (Fig. 4(f)), consistent to C–H/C–C, C–O, and O–CN bonds, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum contained peaks at 531.4, 532.5, and 532.9 eV corresponding to OC–O, O–H, and CO (Fig. 4(g)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BP/NS-CDs as a photoluminescent probe for Cu2+/Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The pH values usually influenced seriously on the sensing abilities. Therefore, the sensing behaviors of BP/NS-CDs for Cu2+/Fe3+ ions were explored by examining the PL change of the maximum emission intensities of BP/NS-CDs and BP/NS-CDs with Cu2+/Fe3+ ions. Fig. S4 presents the PL intensity of BP/NS-CDs at pH values within the range 1–13. In the absence of Cu2+/Fe3+ ions, the PL intensity increases with an increase in the pH value in acidic conditions and then decreases in alkaline conditions, and the PL intensity reaches its maximum value at pH∼7.0. After the addition of Cu2+/Fe3+ ions, the PL quenching occurs and the PL intensity of BP/NS-CDs decreases significantly at all pH values. The PL changes became remarkable, which might be attributed to the protonation of –NH2 group in high acidic system and deprotonation for –OH group in high alkalinity, resulting the destruction of aromatic conjugate system and complex system. These pH ranges were larger than that of usual living environment and was conducive to in vivo application. Therefore, pH∼7.0 is selected to perform the following Cu2+/Fe3+ ions sensing experiments.

To investigate the potential use of BP/NS-CDs as a PL probe, we examined the effects of various metal ions on PL intensity (Fig. 5 (a and b)). Highest BP/NS-CD PL peaks have been previously reported to be noticeably quenched in the presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ (Chandra et al., 2016; M. C. Rong et al., 2017), also this quenching was not affected by the presence of other metal cations. The selectivity of the interaction between BP/NS-CDs and Cu2+ or Fe3+ was visually apparent in solutions containing cations. The bright green PL of BP/NS-CDs under a UV lamp remained unaltered in the presence of all metal ions except Cu2+ and Fe3+ (Fig. 5(c)), which suggested BP/NS-CDs have high selectivity for sensing Cu2+ and Fe3+ due to the PL turn-off effect (He and Liu, 2016). Besides, the quenching kinetics behavior of BP/NS-CDs was also investigated by monitoring the PL intensity as a function of time and the interaction between BP/NS-CDs and Cu2+/Fe3+ ions. The results indicate that the BP/NS-CDs–Cu2+ and BP/NS-CDs–Fe3+ complexes are a fast and effective process where the equilibrium is reached within 3 min (Fig. S5). After reaching the equilibrium, the PL intensity of BP/NS-CDs–Cu2+ and BP/NS-CDs–Fe3+ complexes were measured under the excitation of 450 nm.

To explore the sensitivity of BP/NS-CD PL for detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+, PL spectra of BP/NS-CDs were obtained after adding Cu2+ and Fe3+ at different concentrations (Figs. 6 and 7
). The intensity of PL quenching increased with Cu2+ or Fe3+ concentration (0–50 μM), and PL decreases were quantitatively related to Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. 6(a and b)) and accompanied by considerable PL color shifts (inset in Fig. 6(a and b)), which indicated the BP/NS-CD sensor was capable of detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ at trace levels in water quantitatively and sensitively (Bisauriya et al., 2022; Phan et al., 2022). In addition, Job's plot analyses, which were used to determine binding stoichiometry, indicated that BP/NS-CDs bind with Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in maximum PL intensity with a mole fraction of 0.5 proving a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio (Mohandoss et al., 2021a) for BP/NS-CDs–Cu2+ and BP/NS-CDs–Fe3+ complexes (Fig. 6(c and d)).

The quenching efficiencies (F0/F) of Cu2+ and Fe3+ demonstrated linear relationships in the concentration range 0–50 μM, which followed the Stern-Volmer equation, i.e., F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Cu2+ or Fe3+], where F0 and F are the PL intensity in the absence and presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Mohandoss et al., 2021b). Plots of F0/F versus Cu2+ or Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 50 μM exhibited good linearity with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9970 and 0.9978, respectively, indicating that Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions have a strong static quenching effect. The limit of detection (LOD) found as 0.18 and 0.27 μM, based on LOD = K × SD/S (Mohandoss et al., 2022a) in Fig. 7(c and d). Notably, the LOD of BP/NS-CDs for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in drinking water were much lower than USEPA limits at 20 μM and 5.37 μM, respectively. Tables S1 and S2 summarizes the sensing performances of the recently reported fluorescent sensors for Cu2+ and Fe3+ determination. As can be evidently seen, the sensing performance of the BP/NS-CDs-based photoluminescent sensor is comparable to or even superior in comparison with those in the literature.

According to spectroscopic and electron microscopic data, as-prepared BP/NS-CDs contain numerous active functional groups that can bind to target analytes in a variety of ways and cause dynamic or static quenching, electron transfer, the inner filter effect, or photoinduced electron transfer (Molaei, 2020). BP/NS-CDs displayed significant fluorescence when in an aqueous solution, but this was dramatically reduced with Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions by a charge transfer mechanism, which shows Cu2+ and Fe3+ bind to BP/NS-CDs and alters their surface state, and that the interaction between BP/NS-CDs and Cu2+ or Fe3+ primarily results in static quenching. Linear fitting of the Stern-Volmer plot exhibited a strong linear correlation for Cu2+ and Fe3+ (R2 = 0.9970 and 0.9978, respectively). When the valence band electrons of BP/NS-CDs are promoted into the conduction band, holes are created in the valence band to form electron-hole pairs, which can participate in the quenching process (Xu et al., 2012). Additionally, the paramagnetic properties of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions may play a role in the quenching of BP/NS-CD PL.

Competition tests on interactions between BP/NS-CDs and Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions were conducted in the presence of remaining metal ions (100 μM). None of these metal ions had any discernible influence on the effects of Cu2+ or Fe3+ on PL intensity (Fig. 8 (a and b)), indicating they did not disrupt the chelations of Cu2+ or Fe3+ by BP/NS-CDs, and that BP/NS-CDs act as highly selective PL chemosensors for Cu2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous environments.

Cell cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging experiments were carried out using HCT-116 cells using an MTT assay to assess the feasibility of using BP/NS-CDs for cell imaging (Mohandoss et al., 2022b). When cells were treated with BP/NS-CDs at 50–300 μg/mL for 24 or 48 h, cell viabilities ranged between 97.6 ± 0.9% and 99.4 ± 1.3% (Fig. 9 ), indicating BP/NS-CDs have negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility (Koutsogiannis et al., 2020).

Given the cytotoxicity results obtained, we investigated the ability of BP/NS-CDs to enable the visualization of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in HCT-116 cells under physiological conditions. Briefly, HCT-116 cells were treated for 30 min at RT with BP/NS-CDs (300 μg/mL). Bright green fluorescence under a confocal microscope confirmed the ability of BP/NS-CDs to penetrate cell membranes (Fig. 10 ) (Unnikrishnan et al., 2020). Cells were then treated with Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions (50 μM; 30 min), washed with PBS three times to remove extracellular Cu2+ and Fe3+, and imaging responses were observed. When HCT-116 cells were cultured with Cu2+ or Fe3+ (50 μM) but without BP/NS-CDs negligible fluorescence was observed, and when cultured with BP/NS-CDs alone they exhibited little fluorescence. These cell experiments showed BP/NS-CDs penetrate cell membranes and can be used to label Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in live cells (Kumari et al., 2018; Rong et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of spiked analytes in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the synthesized chemosensor probe BP/NS-CDs was tested by analyzing Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations in tap and lake water samples (Mohandoss et al., 2022a). For this experiment, three distinct concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions were prepared by spiking the standard of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. PL emission intensities obtained correlated with PL emission intensity within the concentration range 1–10 μM.

Analyses were conducted in triplicate, and the results are shown in Table 1 predictable values agreed well. Furthermore, high recoveries (between 83.4 and 114.0%) and low RSD% values (0.02–2.13%) showed that BP/NS-CDs can function as accurate sensors for the determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ levels in real water samples. Notably, BP/NS-CD sensor-determined Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations agreed well with those obtained using the standard detection method.

The photoluminescent boron and phosphorous co-doped NS-CDs were synthesized using a straightforward one-pot hydrothermally assisted method and used as an environmentally friendly, biocompatible sensor for Cu2+ and Fe3+. BP/NS-CDs were found to exhibit good water solubility, excellent photoluminescence stability, high quantum yield, a single graphene structure, high crystallinity, a uniform size distribution with high monodispersity (7.2 ± 0.45 nm), and bright and excitation-dependent emission, PL stability over large pH, and high ionic strength. The optimized synthesis of BP/NS-CDs parameters were found at 180 °C, 12 h and 15% of PBA mass, in which QY was predicted to be 22.4%. The properties of BP/NS-CDs were characterized by UV–vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, TEM(HRTEM), and XPS analysis. We believe that the doping of boron and phosphorus may have changed the surface state of BP/NS-CDs. BP/NS-CDs chemosensors coordinated with Cu2+ and Fe3+ with a stoichiometry of 1:1 and provided highly sensitive and selective PL responses to these two metal ions in the presence of other metal ions in water. This significant PL quenching (with associated color changes) was putatively attributed to coordination between BP/NS-CD and Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions and the paramagnetic effect of spin-orbit coupling of these metal ions. BP/NS-CD sensors had LODs of 0.18 and 0.27 μM for Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, which are far lower than USEPA guidelines for drinking water. Furthermore, the proposed BP/NS-CD photoluminescent probe was found to be suitable for field analysis and the determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations in real samples. Notably, BP/NS-CD “on-off” sensors were suitable for the visualization of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in HCT-116 cells. Thus, we consider the BP/NS-CDs produced have potential applications for cell imaging and environmental monitoring.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Sivarasan Ganesan: Validation & Review editing. Singaravelu Anand: Methodology, Investigation. Alagarasan Jagadeesh Kumar: Methodology, Investigation. Subramanian Palanisamy: Methodology, Investigation. SangGuan You: Methodology, Investigation. K. Velsankar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Sakkarapani Sudhahar: Methodology, Investigation. Huang-Mu Lo: Validation & Review editing. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
